title: Environment variables should not be defined from untrusted input
description: Environment variable injection occurs in an application when the application receives data from a user or a third-party service and, without sanitizing it first, does the following: Creates an environment variable based on the external data. Inserts the external data into certain sensitive environment variables, such as PATH or LD_PRELOAD. If an application uses environment variables that are vulnerable to injection, it is exposed to a variety of attacks that aim to exploit supposedly safe environment variables, such as `PATH`.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

logic.detection_conditions: Environment.SetEnvironmentVariable called with untrusted user input (Request, Console.ReadLine, etc.)
logic.triggering_conditions: CallExpression matching SetEnvironmentVariable AND source contains user input patterns (Request., Console.ReadLine, etc.) AND NOT using hardcoded strings
logic.exclusion_conditions: Exclude calls with hardcoded strings, const variables, validated input, and test code
logic.reasoning: Detects environment variable injection vulnerabilities where untrusted user input is used to set environment variables. This is a CRITICAL security issue that can lead to command injection, privilege escalation, and system compromise. The rule looks for Environment.SetEnvironmentVariable calls with arguments containing user input sources like Request.QueryString, Request.Form, Request.Params, Console.ReadLine, etc. Excludes safe cases with hardcoded values, const variables, and properly validated input. This is a compound check requiring both SetEnvironmentVariable call AND user input presence.
